elements.sent-2411-001: Text-conditioned video diffusion models have emerged as a powerful tool in the realm of video generation and editing.
elements.sent-2411-002: But their ability to capture the nuances of human movement remains under-explored.
elements.sent-2411-003: Indeed the ability of these models to faithfully model an array of text prompts can lead to a wide host of applications in human and character animation.
elements.sent-2411-004: In this work, we take initial steps to investigate whether these models can effectively guide the synthesis of realistic human body animations.
elements.sent-2411-005: Specifically we propose to synthesize human motion by deforming an SMPL-X body representation guided by Score distillation sampling (SDS) calculated using a video diffusion model.
elements.sent-2411-006: By analyzing the fidelity of the resulting animations, we gain insights into the extent to which we can obtain motion using publicly available text-to-video diffusion models using SDS.
elements.sent-2411-007: Our findings shed light on the potential and limitations of these models for generating diverse and plausible human motions, paving the way for further research in this exciting area.
elements.sent-2411-008: Video generative models\cite{imagen_video,villegas2022phenaki,sora,modelscope,videocrafter2,make_a_video have been shown and claimed to be potential tools for simulating the world.}
elements.sent-2411-009: Recent advancements, such as those demonstrated by \cite{sora , highlight the capabilities of video diffusion models trained on vast datasets to generate realistic and diverse visual content.}
elements.sent-2411-010: These impressive capabilities inspired us to question the potential of open-source counterparts for the specific task of generating human motion animations from natural language input.
elements.sent-2411-011: We sought to determine if the current state-of-the-art open-source models can be used with score distillation to generate human motion effectively.
elements.sent-2411-012: Human motion generation from textual instructions is a well-studied domain\cite{action_gul_varol,mdm,avatarone,motionclip , with recent methods often involving diffusion models trained on motion capture (MoCap) data\cite{mdm,priormdm .}}
elements.sent-2411-013: However, the scarcity of MoCap data compared to the abundance of video data presents a scalability challenge.
elements.sent-2411-014: Extracting animation directly from videos, mirroring the success seen in 3D asset generation\cite{get3d,clipmesh,magic3d,prolificdreamer , represents an ambitious yet necessary goal.}
elements.sent-2411-015: We define our problem as determining the correct sequence of joint rotations necessary to produce realistic human motion described by a prompt.
elements.sent-2411-016: To achieve this, we utilize the widely adopted SMPL-X\cite{smplx digital human template model to render a character.}
elements.sent-2411-017: This character is animated through an optimization process that iteratively updates a multi-layer perceptron (MLP) to predict the corresponding pose parameters.
elements.sent-2411-018: We guide this optimization process using a video diffusion model, which provides feedback on the realism of the generated motion.
elements.sent-2411-019: As shown in Figure \ref{fig:teaser , these models demonstrate a proficiency in generating animations for common actions such as running.}
elements.sent-2411-020: However, when tasked with depicting uncommon or rare human movements, their performance under score distillation reveals limitations.
elements.sent-2411-021: While they can produce visually appealing results for familiar actions, their ability to capture the nuances of less frequent movements remains a challenge.
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{images/teaser.png} \caption{Human motion sequence resembling running generated using text-to-video model. The figure illustrates that the current video models can generate realistic motion for commonly occuring human activity such as running} \label{fig:teaser}
elements.sent-2411-022: Our main contributions are as follows:.
elements.sent-2411-023: We propose a differentiable video generation pipeline: MotionDistill that leverages text-to-video diffusion models to generate human motion.
elements.sent-2411-024: We conduct an evaluation of ModelScope\cite{modelscope , ZeroScope\cite{zeroscope , and VideoCrafter\cite{videocrafter1,videocrafter2 's capabilities in generating both common and uncommon human motion.}}}
elements.sent-2411-025: We ablate our analysis to the latent space of these models to test the effectiveness of SDS.
elements.sent-2411-026: Diffusion models in content creation: Recent advances in text-to-image\cite{imagen,stablediffusion foundation models \cite{foundationmodels have catalyzed exploration into their application for three-dimensional (3D) asset synthesis\cite{sivakumar2024fewshotnerf and editing\cite{zamani2024temporally .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-027: Early works, particularly with CLIP\cite{clip -based models , leveraged joint image-text embeddings to generate 3D assets directly from text prompts \cite{clipmesh,avatarone,dreamfields,clip_forge,clip_nerf,dream3d .}}
elements.sent-2411-028: However, the emergence of diffusion models \cite{diffusion_beats_gan,diffusion_model and the introduction of SDS \cite{dreamfusion marked an important shift.}}
elements.sent-2411-029: SDS enabled the extraction of 3D assets\cite{prolificdreamer,dreamfusion,dreamgaussian,magic3d,get3d as NeRFs\cite{nerf , Meshes\cite{dmtet,tada,dreamavatar and Gaussian splats\cite{gaussian_splats .\cite{make_a_video_4d integrated a temporal dimension, facilitating the generation of animated 2D\cite{breathinglife and 3D assets from video diffusion models.\cite{4dfy,dreamin4d,align_your_gaussians , introduced hybrid SDS methodologies and alternative representations like Gaussian splattings \cite{align_your_gaussians to enhance the fidelity and motion quality of generated assets.}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-030: However, these approaches focused on open-ended generation and did not specifically address the potential of video models to generate diverse human motions.
elements.sent-2411-031: Text to Human motion generation: The generation of human motion guided by textual descriptions is a well-established area of research.
elements.sent-2411-032: Initial approaches explored to model it as machine translation \cite{learning_bi_mapping,text2action and joint cross-modal mappings \cite{ghosh2021synthesis,language2pose to address this challenge.}}
elements.sent-2411-033: Subsequent works leveraged motion capture datasets\cite{kitmotionlanguage,amass to train models capable of generating human motion as sequences of poses.}
elements.sent-2411-034: Variational Autoencoders (VAEs)\cite{vae were employed in \cite{petrovich2022temos, guo2022generating .}}
elements.sent-2411-035: The concept of a shared latent space with CLIP\cite{clip was introduced in \cite{motionclip .}}
elements.sent-2411-036: Human motion diffusion model \cite{mdm pioneered the application of diffusion-based modeling to human motion generation, enabling ancestral sampling in the motion space, which subsequently led to the utilization of SDS\cite{dreamfusion in \cite{priormdm for generating extended motion sequences.}}}
elements.sent-2411-037: These prior methods are limited by motion capture data, operating solely in the rotation space.
elements.sent-2411-038: Our approach ventures into the pixel space models, exploring their potential in motion generation.
elements.sent-2411-039: Human template models - SMPLx: The Skinned Multi-person Linear(SMPL)\cite{smpl,smplx,mano,flame family of models comprises articulated human body models parameterized by the shape and pose parameters.}
elements.sent-2411-040: Among these, We use SMPLx because of its comprehensive representation of the human body.
elements.sent-2411-041: It is defined by the function $M(\theta,\beta,\phi): \mathbb{R ^{|\theta| \times |\beta| \times |\psi| \rightarrow \mathbb{R ^{3N}$ \cite{smplx given by Equation \ref{eqn:smplx . \begin{equation}\label{eqn:smplx} M(\beta,\theta,\psi) = LBS(T_p(\beta,\theta,\psi),J(\beta),\theta,\mathcal{W})\end{equation} This function takes body parameters as input, performs linear blend skinning, and outputs a mesh with $N=10,475$ vertices.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-042: Here $\theta \in \mathbb{R ^{3(K+1)}$ denotes the body pose parameters, $\beta \in \mathbb{R ^{|\beta|}$ denotes the shape parameters and $\psi \in \mathbb{R ^{|\psi|}$ denotes the facial expression parameters.}}}
elements.sent-2411-043: The pose parameters $\theta$ can be further divided into $\theta_b$ (body joints pose), $\theta_f$ (jaw pose) and $\theta_h$ (finger pose).
elements.sent-2411-044: While SMPLx accounts for $K=54$ body joints, our approach optimizes only the major body joints $K_{b =21$ focusing exclusively on $\theta_b$ to constraint our scope.}
elements.sent-2411-045: The template body $T_{p (\beta,\theta,\psi)$ is given by Equation \ref{eqn:smplx_template .}}
elements.sent-2411-046: Here $\hat{T}$ is the template mesh, $B_S, B_E, B_P$ denotes the blend shape functions corresponding to shape, expression, and pose. \begin{equation}\label{eqn:smplx_template} T_{p}(\beta,\theta,\psi) = \Bar{T} + B_{S}(\beta;\mathcal{S}) + B_{E}(\psi;\mathcal{\mathcal{E}}) + B_{P}(\theta;\mathcal{P})\end{equation} Score Distillation Sampling: Score Distillation Sampling (SDS)\cite{dreamfusion is a method employed to leverage large-scale diffusion models\cite{ddpm,diffusion_model for training compact parametric image generators.}}
elements.sent-2411-047: It utilizes the score function\cite{scorebasedgen of the diffusion model to derive gradient directions for updating the generator, iteratively aligning it with provided textual prompts.}
elements.sent-2411-048: Our formulation of SDS slightly deviates from the original by employing a latent diffusion model\cite{ldm .Given a pre-trained latent diffusion model $\phi$ with its denoising UNet $\hat{\epsilon _{\phi (z_t;y,t)$ \cite{unet , text prompt $y$ and an image generator parameterized by $\theta$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-049: The gradients needed to update the generator are obtained by Equation \ref{eqn:sds .}
elements.sent-2411-050: Here $t$ is the randomly sampled diffusion timestep, and $\epsilon$ is the randomly generated noise.
elements.figure-2411-002: \includegraphics[width=1.0\textwidth]{images/method.png} \caption{Our study consists of two stages. Stage:1 (top) Joint rotations required to animate the character are generated using PoseField. Passed through SMPLx Layer to get the final mesh which is then rasterized using a differentiable renderer. We use a random camera and a predetermined texture. This is repeated for F frames to obtain the video. Stage:2 (bottom) Rendered video is encoded to the latent space of the diffusion model then random noise is added to the latent. Unet of the Video model is used to predict the added noise and the gradients are estimated by SDS.} \label{fig:method}
elements.sent-2411-051: In this section, we detail our proposed methodology.
elements.sent-2411-052: The central challenge we address is as follows: Given a text prompt $y$ describing an action, we aim to determine the optimal joint rotations $\theta_{b}$ for each frame $\{\theta_{b_{1 ,\theta_{b_{2 ,...}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-053: To tackle this, we model the rotations implicitly using a neural network "PoseField" $P_{\alpha :\tau \rightarrow \theta_b$ parameterized by $\alpha$.}
elements.sent-2411-054: It is a Multi-Layer Perceptron (MLP) comprising two hidden layers.
elements.sent-2411-055: This network takes the frame id $\tau$ as input and outputs the corresponding body pose parameter $\theta_b$.
elements.sent-2411-056: Consequently, our task shifts from finding the optimal rotation set to determining the optimal parameters $\alpha^{*}$ of the network $P_\alpha$.
elements.sent-2411-057: As illustrated in Figure \ref{fig:method , our approach employs a two-stage process.}
elements.sent-2411-058: Video generation: We utilize a differentiable pipeline to synthesize a video of the animation sequence.
elements.sent-2411-059: Gradient Estimation: We leverage video diffusion models to estimate gradients for updating the PoseField parameters.
elements.subsection-2411-001: Stage 1: Generating multi-view video of the animation sequence
elements.sent-2411-060: In this stage, we generate the video of the animation sequence frame by frame and subsequently concatenate these frames to form a complete video.
elements.sent-2411-061: We encode the frame identifier $\tau$ with positional encoding\cite{transformer and infer the PoseField $P_{\alpha}$ to obtain the SMPL-X body pose parameter $\boldsymbol{\theta _{b = P_{\alpha (\tau)$ corresponding to the current frame.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-062: Using this, we derive the mesh $M(\boldsymbol{\beta , [P_{\alpha (\tau), \boldsymbol{\theta _f, \boldsymbol{\theta _h], \boldsymbol{\psi )$.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-063: It is important to note that only $\alpha$ is the trainable parameter here, while all other values are reused across frames and training iterations.
elements.sent-2411-064: We render the character from a randomly selected camera position sampled from a circular trajectory around the mesh.
elements.sent-2411-065: Given the camera trajectory $C$ and the rendering function $R$, we obtain the projection matrix $\pi$ and render the current frame as $I_{\alpha = R(\pi(M))$.}
elements.sent-2411-066: This process is repeated for $F$ frames, generating a series of frames ${I_1, I_2, \ldots, I_F}$.
elements.sent-2411-067: We concatenate these frames to obtain the video $V_{\alpha}$ from a certain view that will be used as input to the next stage for gradient estimation.
elements.sent-2411-068: The generated video is then used to estimate the gradient using Score Distillation Sampling (SDS)\cite{dreamfusion .}
elements.sent-2411-069: We employ the temporal variation of SDS as proposed in \cite{make_a_video_4d .}
elements.sent-2411-070: Since we use Latent Diffusion Models, we first encode the video to the latent space of the video diffusion model: $Z_{\alpha = E(V_{\alpha )$.}}
elements.sent-2411-071: We then add noise to the video latent according to the noise schedule: $Z_{(\alpha,\sigma,\epsilon) = \sqrt{1-\sigma^{2 Z_\alpha + \sigma\epsilon$, where $\epsilon$ is randomly generated noise and $\sigma \in (0,1)$ is the noise level.}}}
elements.sent-2411-072: Gradients are then computed by Equation \ref{eqn:sds_t .}
elements.sent-2411-073: Here $\lambda_{SDS_t}$ is a hyperparameter that controls the effect of SDS. \begin{equation}\label{eqn:sds_t} \nabla_{\theta}\mathcal{L}_{SDS-T} = \lambda_{SDS_t} E_{\sigma,\epsilon}[w(\sigma) \hat{\epsilon}(Z_{\alpha,\sigma,\epsilon}|y,\sigma) - \epsilon) \frac{\partial Z_{\alpha}}{\partial \alpha}]\end{equation} Regularization: To encourage smoothness in the generated motion, we introduce a regularization constraint.
elements.sent-2411-074: We add the following loss term, which minimizes the difference between the body poses of consecutive frames.
elements.sent-2411-075: Here $\lambda_{reg}$ is a regularization coefficient. \begin{equation}\mathcal{L}_{reg} = \lambda_{reg} \sum_{i=1}^{F-1} (\theta_{b_{i+1}} - \theta_{b_{i}})\end{equation} SDS from Stable diffusion model: Following recent works\cite{align_your_gaussians,4dfy,make_a_video_4d and considering the fact that video diffusion models often lack visual quality compared to their image counterparts, we additionally estimate gradients using the standard SDS with an Image Diffusion Model.}
elements.sent-2411-076: We treat each video as a batch of images and calculate the gradients using Equation \ref{eqn:sds .}
elements.figure-2411-003: \includegraphics[width=0.9\textwidth]{images/results.png} \caption{Our results for different motions: All results are obtained by using the model VideoCrafter\cite{videocrafter2}. (i) Walking motion is one of the best cases in addition to running (Fig:\ref{fig:teaser}). (ii) Punching is a semi-failure case (iii) Cartwheel is an extreme failure case. } \label{fig:results}
elements.sent-2411-077: Evaluating MotionDistill: Figure \ref{fig:results showcases the capabilities of our method across various prompts.}
elements.sent-2411-078: Our approach excels at generating plausible motion for common human activities, as demonstrated by the "walking" and "running" examples (Figure \ref{fig:results (i) and \ref{fig:teaser ).}}
elements.sent-2411-079: However, challenges arise with less frequent actions.
elements.sent-2411-080: The "punching" prompt (Figure \ref{fig:results (ii)) resulted in partial motion generation, with limited hand movement.}
elements.sent-2411-081: This shows for a semi-failure case.
elements.sent-2411-082: Furthermore, the model struggles with uncommon activities like "doing a cartwheel" (Figure \ref{fig:results (iii)), failing to produce even a reasonable pose giving the extreme failure case \footnote{Check supplementary video at \href{https://github.com/Pauljanson002/human-eccv {https://github.com/Pauljanson002/human-eccv for sample clips .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-083: Ablating MotionDistill: It is challenging to determine if the failure cases comes from the SDS, the faithfulness of the video diffusion model, or the way we're representing motion and render it To gain deeper insights into the ability of SDS and our video models to produce faithful motion, we optimize our SDS objective with the video diffusion model directly in their video latent space.
elements.sent-2411-084: We first render the initial video using Stage 1 of our pipeline.
elements.sent-2411-085: Then detach this rendering from the optimization process and directly optimize the latents $\boldsymbol{Z}$.
elements.sent-2411-086: The results are shown in Figure \ref{fig:latent .}
elements.sent-2411-087: Our experiments reveal performance discrepancies among three open-source video diffusion models, particularly concerning the generation of common versus rare human motions.
elements.sent-2411-088: This suggests a potential bias towards common activities in the training data.
elements.sent-2411-089: To illustrate this, we used two prompts for all three models: "running" (a common action) and "punching" (less frequent).
elements.sent-2411-090: As shown in Figure \ref{fig:latent , the models generated more natural motion sequences for "running." In contrast, the "punching" frames lacked variation, highlighting the limitations of all three models with less common motions.}
elements.sent-2411-091: We also observed that recent models\cite{videocrafter2 provide a much more realistic motion than older models\cite{modelscope,zeroscope .}}
elements.sent-2411-092: This is evident, if we compare the top rows of each model, ModelScope\cite{modelscope seems to have very little variation across frames even for "running".}
elements.sent-2411-093: Indeed we observe when the model has challenges with the latent generation it also struggles in the case of fitting PoseField, suggesting the issue lies not in the representation of the body motion but in the video diffusion model.
elements.sent-2411-094: Implementation details: We implemented the pipeline in PyTorch\cite{pytorch and used nvdiffrast\cite{nvdiffrast for differentiable rendering.}}
elements.sent-2411-095: We conducted experiments in NVIDIA A6000 GPU.
elements.sent-2411-096: We used Adam\cite{adam optimizer with a learning rate of 5e-4 for 10,000 iterations.}
elements.sent-2411-097: Experiments maintained a CFG\cite{cfg scale of 100.}
elements.sent-2411-098: We initialized the PoseField to output the mean pose of the target motion and constrained its output within a range defined by three times the standard deviation of the target pose parameters.
elements.figure-2411-004: \includegraphics[width=0.95\textwidth]{images/latent.png} \caption{Visualization of the optimized latent when given the two actions as prompts. Generated videos in the top row of each video model denote the action "running". The bottom row of each denotes the action "punching". Clearly, the top rows of each model show a more natural motion. VideoCrafter\cite{videocrafter2} demonstrates a higher degree of realism in both actions compared to other models} \label{fig:latent}
elements.sent-2411-100: Video diffusion models can generate human motion from text, but their performance differs a lot between familiar and rare actions.
elements.sent-2411-101: Notably, more recent models like VideoCrafter2\cite{videocrafter2 outperform earlier ones\cite{modelscope,zeroscope .}}
elements.sent-2411-102: These findings underscore the need for further research to improve the diversity and quality of human motion generation, particularly for less frequent or complex actions.
elements.sent-2411-103: We hypothesize that our method when combined with more powerful text-to-video foundation models can become increasingly more effective.
elements.sent-2411-104: Our work stands as proof of concept in this domain and studies the strengths and limitations of current open-source video diffusion models.
elements.sent-2411-105: Additionally, studying human motion understanding as an emergent behavior of a video diffusion model will be a promising future direction.. \bibliographystyle{splncs04.}

elements.sent-2411-151: ECCV reviewing is double blind, in that authors do not know the names of the area chair/reviewers of their papers, and the area chairs/reviewers cannot, beyond reasonable doubt, infer the names of the authors from the submission and the additional material.
elements.sent-2411-152: You must not identify the authors nor provide links to websites that identify the authors, neither in the paper nor in the supplemental material.
elements.sent-2411-153: If you need to cite a different paper of yours that is being submitted concurrently to ECCV, you should (1) cite these papers anonymously, (2) argue in the body of your paper why your ECCV paper is non-trivially different from these concurrent submissions, and (3) include anonymized versions of those papers in the supplemental material.
elements.sent-2411-154: Violation of any of these guidelines may lead to rejection without review.
elements.sent-2411-155: Many authors misunderstand the concept of anonymizing for blind review.
elements.sent-2411-156: Blind review does not mean that one must remove citations to one's own work---in fact it is often impossible to review a paper unless the previous citations are known and available.
elements.sent-2411-157: Blind review means that you do not use the words ``my'' or ``our'' when citing previous work.. That is all.
elements.sent-2411-158: If you are Smith and Jones, do not say ``as we show in [7]'', say ``as Smith and Jones show in [7]'' and at the end of the paper, include reference 7 as you would any other cited work.
elements.sent-2411-159: An example of a bad paper just asking to be rejected:.
elements.sent-2411-160: Why the previous paper was accepted without this analysis is beyond me.
elements.sent-2411-161: An example of an acceptable paper:.
elements.sent-2411-162: If you are making a submission to another conference at the same time, which covers similar or overlapping material, you may need to refer to that submission in order to explain the differences, just as you would if you had previously published related work.
elements.sent-2411-163: In such cases, include the anonymized parallel submission [1] as supplemental material and cite it as.
elements.sent-2411-164: Finally, you may feel you need to tell the reader that more details can be found elsewhere, and refer them to a technical report.
elements.sent-2411-165: For conference submissions, the paper must stand on its own, and not require the reviewer to go to a tech report for further details.
elements.sent-2411-166: Thus, you may say in the body of the paper ``further details may be found in~\cite{Anonymous24b ''.}
elements.sent-2411-167: Then submit the tech report as supplemental material.
elements.sent-2411-168: Again, you may not assume the reviewers will read this material.
elements.sent-2411-169: Sometimes your paper is about a problem, which you tested using a tool that is widely known to be restricted to a single institution.
elements.sent-2411-170: For example, let's say it's 1969, you have solved a key problem on the Apollo lander, and you believe that the ECCV audience would like to hear about your solution.
elements.sent-2411-171: The work is a development of your celebrated 1968 paper entitled ``Zero-g frobnication: How being the only people in the world with access to the Apollo lander source code makes us a wow at parties'', by Zeus \etal.
elements.sent-2411-172: You can handle this paper like any other.
elements.sent-2411-173: Do not write ``We show how to improve our previous work [Anonymous, 1968].
elements.sent-2411-174: This time we tested the algorithm on a lunar lander [name of lander removed for blind review]''.
elements.sent-2411-175: That would be silly, and would immediately identify the authors.
elements.sent-2411-177: We describe a system for zero-g frobnication.
elements.sent-2411-178: This system is new because it handles the following cases: A, B.
elements.sent-2411-179: Previous systems [Zeus et al. 1968] did not handle case B properly.
elements.sent-2411-180: Ours handles it by including a foo term in the bar integral.. ...
elements.sent-2411-181: The proposed system was integrated with the Apollo lunar lander, and went all the way to the moon, don't you know.

elements.sent-2411-184: A reviewer might think it likely that the new paper was written by Zeus \etal, but cannot make any decision based on that guess.
elements.sent-2411-185: He or she would have to be sure that no other authors could have been contracted to solve problem B.
elements.sent-2411-186: For sake of anonymity, authors must omit acknowledgements in the review copy.
elements.sent-2411-187: They can be added later when you prepare the final copy.
elements.sent-2411-188: Headings should be capitalized (\ie, nouns, verbs, and all other words except articles, prepositions, and conjunctions should be set with an initial capital) and should, with the exception of the title, be aligned to the left.
elements.sent-2411-189: Only the first two levels of section headings should be numbered, as shown in \cref{tab:headings .}
elements.sent-2411-190: The respective font sizes are also given in \cref{tab:headings .}
elements.sent-2411-191: Kindly refrain from using ``0'' when numbering your section headings.
elements.sent-2411-192: Words joined by a hyphen are subject to a special rule.
elements.sent-2411-193: If the first word can stand alone, the second word should be capitalized.
elements.figure-2411-005: \caption{Font sizes of headings. Table captions should always be positioned above the tables. } \label{tab:headings} \begin{tabular}{@{}lll@{}} Heading level Example Font size and style Title (centered) { \bf Lecture Notes \dots} 14 point, bold 1st-level heading { \bf 1 Introduction} 12 point, bold 2nd-level heading {\bf 2.1 Printing Area} 10 point, bold 3rd-level heading {\bf Headings.} Text follows \dots 10 point, bold 4th-level heading {\it Remark.} Text follows \dots 10 point, italic \end{tabular}
elements.sent-2411-194: Here are some examples of headings: ``Criteria to Disprove Context-Freeness of Collage Languages'', ``On Correcting the Intrusion of Tracing Non-deterministic Programs by Software'', ``A User-Friendly and Extendable Data Distribution System'', ``Multi-flip Networks: Parallelizing GenSAT'', ``Self-determinations of Man''.
elements.sent-2411-195: For \LaTeX{ users, we recommend integrating figures in your paper using the package graphicx.}
elements.sent-2411-196: It is essential that all illustrations are clear and legible.
elements.sent-2411-197: Vector graphics (rather than rasterized images) should be used for diagrams and schemas whenever possible.
elements.sent-2411-198: Please check that the lines in line drawings are not interrupted and have a constant width.
elements.sent-2411-199: Line drawings are to have a resolution of at least 800 dpi (preferably 1200 dpi).
elements.sent-2411-200: Grids and details within figures must be clearly legible and may not be written one on top of the other.
elements.sent-2411-201: The lettering in figures should not use font sizes smaller than 6\:pt ($\sim$2\:mm character height).
elements.sent-2411-202: Figures should be numbered and should have a caption, which should always be positioned under the figures, in contrast to the caption belonging to a table, which should always appear above the table.
elements.sent-2411-203: Figures and Tables should be cross-referred in the text.
elements.sent-2411-204: If they are short, they are centered between the margins (\cf \cref{fig:short ).}
elements.sent-2411-205: Longer captions, covering more than one line, are justified (\cref{fig:example shows an example).}
elements.sent-2411-206: Captions that do not constitute a full sentence, do not have a period.
elements.figure-2411-006: \includegraphics[height=6.5cm]{eijkel2} \caption{One kernel at $x_s$ (dotted kernel) or two kernels at $x_i$ and $x_j$ (left and right) lead to the same summed estimate at $x_s$. This shows a figure consisting of different types of lines. Elements of the figure described in the caption should be set in italics, in parentheses, as shown in this sample caption. The last sentence of a figure caption should generally end with a full stop, except when the caption is not a full sentence. } \label{fig:example}
elements.figure-2411-007: \begin{subfigure}{0.68\linewidth} \fbox{\rule{0pt}{0.5in} \rule{.9\linewidth}{0pt}} \caption{An example of a subfigure} \label{fig:short-a} \end{subfigure} \begin{subfigure}{0.28\linewidth} \fbox{\rule{0pt}{0.5in} \rule{.9\linewidth}{0pt}} \caption{Another example of a subfigure} \label{fig:short-b} \end{subfigure} \caption{Centered, short example caption} \label{fig:short}
elements.sent-2411-207: If possible (\eg, if you use \LaTeX) please define figures as floating objects.
elements.sent-2411-208: If you have to insert a pagebreak before a figure, please ensure that the previous page is completely filled.
elements.sent-2411-209: Displayed equations or formulas are centered and set on a separate line (with an extra line or half line space above and below).
elements.sent-2411-210: Equations should be numbered for reference.
elements.sent-2411-211: The numbers should be consecutive within the contribution, with numbers enclosed in parentheses and set on the right margin.
elements.sent-2411-212: For example, \begin{equation}\psi (u) = \int_{0}^{T} \left[\frac{1}{2} \left(\Lambda_{0}^{-1} u,u\right) + N^{\ast} (-u)\right] dt \; = 0\end{equation} and \begin{equation}E = m\cdot c^2. \label{eq:important}\end{equation} Please do not include section counters in the numbering.
elements.sent-2411-213: Numbering equations makes reviewing more efficient, because reviewers can refer to a line on a page.
elements.sent-2411-214: It is important for readers to be able to refer to any particular equation.
elements.sent-2411-215: Just because you did not refer to it in the text does not mean some future reader might not need to refer to it.
elements.sent-2411-216: It is cumbersome to have to use circumlocutions like ``the equation second from the top of page 3''.
elements.sent-2411-217: All authors will benefit from reading Mermin's description of how to write mathematics: \url{https://doi.org/10.1063/1.2811173 .}
elements.sent-2411-218: Equations should never be in color and should be punctuated in the same way as ordinary text.
elements.sent-2411-219: They should not be pasted in as figures.
elements.sent-2411-220: The numbers accorded to lemmas, propositions, and theorems, \etc should appear in consecutive order, starting with Lemma 1.
elements.sent-2411-221: Please do not include section counters in the numbering like ``Theorem 1.1''.
elements.sent-2411-222: The superscript numeral used to refer to a footnote appears in the text either directly after the word to be discussed or -- in relation to a phrase or a sentence -- following the punctuation mark (comma, semicolon, or period).
elements.sent-2411-223: Second and subsequent lines are indented.
elements.sent-2411-224: For remarks pertaining to the title or the authors' names, in the header of a paper, symbols should be used instead of a number.
elements.sent-2411-225: Please note that no footnotes may be included in the abstract.

elements.sent-2411-230: If you do not wish to abbreviate the label, for example at the beginning of the sentence, you can use the.
elements.sent-2411-232: Program listings or program commands in the text are normally set in typewriter font (\eg, printf("Hello world!\textbackslash{n"); ).}
elements.sent-2411-233: Arabic numbers are used for citation, which is sequential either by order of citation or by alphabetical order of the references, depending on which sequence is used in the list of references.
elements.sent-2411-234: The reference numbers are given in brackets and are not superscript.
elements.sent-2411-238: The numbers should be listed in numerical order.
elements.sent-2411-239: If you use the template as advised, this will be taken care of automatically.
elements.sent-2411-240: If an author's name is used in the text: Alpher \cite{Alpher02 was the first \ldots.}
elements.sent-2411-241: Please write all references using the Latin alphabet.
elements.sent-2411-242: If the title of the book you are referring to is, \eg, in Russian or Chinese, then please write (in Russian) or (in Chinese) at the end of the transcript or translation of the title.
elements.sent-2411-243: All references cited in the text should be in the list of references and vice versa.
elements.sent-2411-244: References should be formatted with the official LNCS reference style.
elements.sent-2411-245: The \LaTeX{ template already takes care of that through the use of the splncs04.bst Bib\TeX{ style file.}}
elements.sent-2411-246: Springer strongly encourages you to include DOIs (Digital Object Identifiers) in your references (\cf \cite{ECCV2022 ).}
elements.sent-2411-247: The DOI is a unique code allotted by the publisher to each online paper or journal article.
elements.sent-2411-248: It provides a stable way of finding published papers and their metadata.
elements.sent-2411-249: The insertion of DOIs increases the overall length of the references section, but this should not concern you as the reference section is not counted toward the page limit.

elements.sent-2411-251: The space after \eg, meaning ``for example'', should not be a sentence-ending space.
elements.sent-2411-252: So \eg is correct, e.g. is not.
elements.sent-2411-253: The provided \verb'\eg' macro takes care of this.
elements.sent-2411-254: When citing a multi-author paper, you may save space by using ``et alia'', shortened to ``\etal'' (not ``{\em et.\ al. '' as ``{\em et '' is a complete word).}}
